x: 7 ways to detect people with just your macbook - fnsmdehip fnsmdehip home uaf series pemf series wifi sensing macbook detection projects health contents the situation the tier system 1. arp / ping scanning 2. rssi monitoring 3. probe request sniffing 4. wifi fingerprinting 5. router-based detection 6. ble scanning 7. why csi doesn't work full tools table security concerns what to actually use 7 ways to detect people with just your macbook 2026-03-28 · series: wifi sensing no esp32, no raspberry pi, no extra hardware. just your laptop and python. this is the companion piece to wifi can see you move , which covers the full landscape including dedicated hardware. this article focuses exclusively on what you can do with a stock macbook. the situation you want to detect whether someone is in a room, in your house, or near your computer. maybe it's for home automation. maybe it's a security project. maybe you're just curious what your wifi adapter can actually do. the good news: your macbook has a wifi adapter, a bluetooth radio, and a full network stack. that's enough for six out of seven detection methods. the bad news: the one method that actually works best (csi extraction) is the one apple's locked you out of. here's everything else, with working code. the recommended tier system not all detection methods are equal. here's how they stack up for practical use: tier method detects accuracy setup time best wifi fingerprinting which room you're in 85-95% 30 min training good ble scanning nearby devices 80-90% 5 min good arp scanning devices on network 95%+ for connected 2 min okay rssi monitoring movement near laptop 60-75% 5 min okay router-based devices on network 90%+ varies limited probe sniffing nearby wifi devices 50-70% 10 min + caveats n/a csi extraction n/a on mac n/a not possible 1. arp / ping scanning the simplest approach. if someone's phone is on your wifi, they're home. send arp requests to every ip on your subnet and see who answers. what you're actually doing arp (address resolution protocol) maps ip addresses to mac addresses on a local network. when you send an arp request for 192.168.1.42, the device at that ip responds with its mac address. by sweeping the whole subnet, you discover every active device. this tells you "a device is connected" not "a person is present." someone can leave their phone charging and go out. a smart tv is always connected. you need to know which mac addresses belong to people's personal devices and filter accordingly. working code #!/usr/bin/env python3 """arp presence scanner. requires: pip3 install scapy""" import time, json from datetime import datetime from scapy.all import arp, ether, srp # map mac addresses to people (fill in your own) known_devices = { "aa:bb:cc:dd:ee:f1": "alice iphone", "aa:bb:cc:dd:ee:f2": "bob pixel", "aa:bb:cc:dd:ee:f3": "alice macbook", } subnet = "192.168.1.0/24" interval = 30 # seconds between scans def scan(subnet): """return set of (ip, mac) tuples for responding devices.""" ans, _ = srp( ether(dst="ff:ff:ff:ff:ff:ff") / arp(pdst=subnet), timeout=3, verbose=false ) return {(r.psrc, r.hwsrc) for _, r in ans} def main(): print(f"scanning {subnet} every {interval}s...") prev_people = set() while true: devices = scan(subnet) current_people = set() for ip, mac in devices: mac_lower = mac.lower() if mac_lower in known_devices: current_people.add(known_devices[mac_lower]) arrived = current_people - prev_people departed = prev_people - current_people ts = datetime.now().strftime("%h:%m:%s") if arrived: print(f"[{ts}] arrived: {', '.join(arrived)}") if departed: print(f"[{ts}] left: {', '.join(departed)}") if not arrived and not departed: who = ', '.join(current_people) if current_people else "nobody" print(f"[{ts}] present: {who} ({len(devices)} devices total)") prev_people = current_people time.sleep(interval) if __name__ == "__main__": main() with nmap (no python needed) # quick scan - who's on the network right now? nmap -sn 192.168.1.0/24 # just mac addresses and ips, fast nmap -sn 192.168.1.0/24 | grep -b1 "mac address" # continuous monitoring (runs every 60 seconds) while true; do echo "=== $(date) ===" nmap -sn 192.168.1.0/24 --open | grep "nmap scan" sleep 60 done limitations only detects devices actively connected to your wifi phone in airplane mode = invisible guest's phone not on your wifi = invisible can't distinguish "phone is here" from "person is here" some devices don't respond to arp when in sleep mode scapy requires root on macos: sudo python3 scanner.py 2. rssi monitoring your macbook constantly measures the signal strength of its wifi connection. that number (rssi, in dbm) changes when the physical environment between your laptop and router changes. when you walk between them, signal drops. when you leave, it recovers. if you track the fluctuation pattern, you can detect movement. how sensitive is it? in a typical home setup with laptop 4-5 meters from the router, a person walking through the line of sight causes a 3-8 dbm dip lasting 1-3 seconds. someone sitting down in the same room causes a sustained 1-3 dbm offset. these numbers are small but statistically distinguishable from the normal 0.5-1 dbm noise floor if you accumulate enough samples. the standard deviation of rssi over a sliding window is the key metric. in an empty room, stdev stays below 1.0 dbm. when someone moves through, it spikes to 2.0-5.0+. a threshold of 1.5-2.0 catches most real movement while ignoring noise. working code #!/usr/bin/env python3 """rssi motion detector for macos. uses the airport utility to read signal strength. no additional packages needed.""" import subprocess, time, statistics, sys from collections import deque airport = ("/system/library/privateframeworks/apple80211.framework" "/versions/current/resources/airport") window = 20 # samples in sliding window poll_hz = 2 # readings per second motion_thresh = 2.0 # stdev threshold for motion idle_thresh = 1.2 # stdev threshold to return to idle def get_rssi(): """read current rssi from airport utility.""" try: r = subprocess.run([airport, "-i"], capture_output=true, text=true, timeout=5) for line in r.stdout.splitlines(): if "agrctlrssi" in line: return int(line.split(":")[1].strip()) except exception: pass return none def get_ssid(): """read current ssid.""" try: r = subprocess.run([airport, "-i"], capture_output=true, text=true, timeout=5) for line in r.stdout.splitlines(): stripped = line.strip() if stripped.startswith("ssid:"): return stripped.split(":", 1)[1].strip() except exception: pass return "unknown" def main(): ssid = get_ssid() print(f"monitoring rssi on '{ssid}' at {poll_hz} hz") print(f"window: {window} samples | motion threshold: {motion_thresh} dbm stdev") print("-" * 60) buf = deque(maxlen=window) state = "idle" motion_count = 0 idle_count = 0 while true: rssi = get_rssi() if rssi is none: print(" [no signal]") time.sleep(1.0 / poll_hz) continue buf.append(rssi) if len(buf) >= 8: mean = statistics.mean(buf) sd = statistics.stdev(buf) rng = max(buf) - min(buf) # state machine with hysteresis if state == "idle" and sd > motion_thresh: motion_count += 1 if motion_count >= 3: # debounce: 3 consecutive readings state = "motion" motion_count = 0 elif state == "motion" and sd = 5: # slower to go idle (avoid flicker) state = "idle" idle_count = 0 else: if state == "idle": motion_count = 0 else: idle_count = 0 bar = "#" * int(sd * 5) print(f" rssi {rssi:4d} dbm | mean {mean:6.1f} | " f"sd {sd:5.2f} | range {rng:2d} | " f"{state:6s} {bar}") else: print(f" rssi {rssi:4d} dbm | buffering ({len(buf)}/{window})...") time.sleep(1.0 / poll_hz) if __name__ == "__main__": main() tuning tips place your macbook so the router is on the other side of the room you're monitoring. person walks between them = maximum signal disruption. 2.4 ghz is more sensitive to body movement than 5 ghz (longer wavelength, more diffraction around obstacles). if your router supports both, force your mac to the 2.4 ghz network for this. the threshold needs calibration per environment. run for 5 minutes in an empty room, note the max stdev, set your threshold 50% above that. microwave ovens operating on 2.4 ghz will cause massive rssi spikes. account for this or switch to 5 ghz. limitations only detects movement near the line between your laptop and router cannot detect stationary presence (someone sitting still) high false positive rate compared to csi-based methods environmental noise (hvac, appliances, other wifi traffic) creates baseline drift single rssi number vs. 64 subcarriers in csi = massively less information 3. probe request sniffing wifi devices periodically broadcast probe requests: "hey, is myhomenetwork nearby?" your macbook's wifi adapter can listen for these if you put it in monitor mode. each probe contains the device's mac address and sometimes the network name it's looking for. the macos caveats this is where things get complicated on mac. modern macos severely limits monitor mode. the built-in airport utility can capture in monitor mode, but: it disconnects you from wifi while monitoring (you can't be connected and in monitor mode simultaneously) apple's newer chips (m1/m2/m3/m4) have inconsistent monitor mode support you need to run as root capture quality varies by macos version # enable monitor mode (disconnects wifi) sudo airport en0 sniff 6 # channel 6 # this creates a pcap file in /tmp/ # parse it after capture: tcpdump -r /tmp/airportsniffxxxxxx.cap \ -e 'type mgt subtype probe-req' \ -l 2>/dev/null | \ grep -oe '(sa|da):[0-9a-f:]+' | sort -u the bigger problem: mac randomization . since ios 14 and android 10, devices randomize their mac address in probe requests. your iphone sends probes with a different fake mac each time. you can't reliably track "alice's phone" across sessions because the identifier changes. some devices still leak their real mac in directed probes (when probing for a specific ssid they've connected to before), but this is increasingly rare. apple and google have been tightening randomization with each os update. practical value in 2026 low. mac randomization has made probe request tracking unreliable for identifying specific people. you can still detect "some wifi device is nearby" but not whose it is. combined with the monitor mode limitations on macos, this approach is more academic than practical. 4. wifi fingerprinting (the best option) this is the most useful laptop-only approach. the idea: every room in your house has a unique pattern of wifi signal strengths from all visible access points. your living room might see your router at -45 dbm and the neighbor's at -72 dbm. your bedroom might see yours at -58 dbm and the neighbor's at -65 dbm. train a classifier on these patterns and it can tell you which room your laptop is in. whereami whereami (~5k github stars) is the easiest implementation. it's a python cli that handles scanning, training, and classification. # install pip3 install whereami # train: stand in each room and record samples whereami learn -l "living_room" # records ~10 scans whereami learn -l "bedroom" whereami learn -l "kitchen" whereami learn -l "office" # predict: where am i right now? whereami predict # output: office (0.87 confidence) # continuous monitoring watch -n 5 whereami predict how it works under the hood whereami scans all visible access points using the os wifi api (corewlan on macos). each scan produces a vector: [ap1_rssi, ap2_rssi, ..., apn_rssi]. it trains a random forest classifier on labeled vectors. at prediction time, it scans again and asks the classifier which room this vector most closely matches. the accuracy depends on three things: number of visible aps: more aps = more dimensions = better separation. in a typical apartment with 5-10 visible networks, expect 85-90% accuracy. in a dense urban area with 20+ networks, 95%+. training quality: record at least 10 scans per room. move around the room between scans. train at different times of day (signal conditions shift slightly). room separation: adjacent rooms with thin walls are hardest to distinguish. rooms on different floors are easiest. find3: the multi-device version find3 (~4.7k stars) extends this concept to track multiple devices. it runs a server that receives fingerprints from multiple clients (phones, laptops, any wifi device running the scanning agent). the server maintains a location model for each device. # server (runs on your local machine or a raspberry pi) docker run -p 8003:8003 schollz/find3 # client (on the device you want to track) # find3-cli-scanner is available for macos, linux, windows find3-cli-scanner -i en0 \ -server http://localhost:8003 \ -family "myhouse" \ -device "macbook" \ -scantime 3 \ -learn "office" # during training # omit -learn for prediction mode limitations detects location, not motion. you know which room the laptop is in, not whether someone else just walked in. requires the laptop to be open and awake. accuracy degrades when the wifi environment changes (new router, neighbor moves). retraining needed periodically (monthly in stable environments, weekly in dynamic ones). 5. router-based detection your router knows exactly which devices are connected, when they connected, when they disconnected, and how much data they're using. if you can query this information programmatically, you have a presence detection system. the approach most routers expose a web interface. many expose an api. the quality varies enormously by brand: router api quality method openwrt excellent ubus json-rpc, luci rest api unifi good rest api with auth token asus (merlin) good http api, snmp tp-link poor screen-scraping the web ui isp-provided terrible usually locked down entirely #!/usr/bin/env python3 """query an openwrt router for connected devices.""" import requests, json router = "http://192.168.1.1" # get auth token (replace with your credentials) auth = requests.post(f"{router}/cgi-bin/luci/rpc/auth", json={ "id": 1, "method": "login", "params": ["root", "your_password"] }) token = auth.json()["result"] # list connected wifi clients clients = requests.post( f"{router}/cgi-bin/luci/rpc/sys?auth={token}", json={"id": 2, "method": "wifi.getassoclist", "params": []} ) for client in clients.json().get("result", []): print(f" mac: {client['mac']} signal: {client.get('signal', '?')} dbm") practical notes if you have an openwrt or unifi router, this is the easiest zero-setup option. you don't even need to run code on your mac continuously. set up a cron job on the router itself (openwrt supports this natively) or poll from your mac every 30 seconds. the main limitation is the same as arp scanning: you're detecting devices, not people. and the update frequency is router-limited. some routers only refresh their client list every few minutes. 6. ble scanning your macbook has a bluetooth radio that can scan for ble (bluetooth low energy) advertisements. modern phones, watches, earbuds, and fitness trackers all broadcast ble advertisements periodically, even when not actively paired to anything. working code with bleak #!/usr/bin/env python3 """ble presence scanner. requires: pip3 install bleak""" import asyncio from bleak import bleakscanner from datetime import datetime # known device names or partial names to track known_devices = { "alice watch", # apple watch "galaxy buds", # samsung earbuds "bob airpods", } async def scan_loop(): print("scanning for ble devices...") known_nearby = set() while true: devices = await bleakscanner.discover(timeout=5.0) current = set() ts = datetime.now().strftime("%h:%m:%s") for d in devices: name = d.name or "unnamed" rssi = d.rssi # check if it's a known device for known in known_devices: if known.lower() in name.lower(): current.add(known) if known not in known_nearby: print(f"[{ts}] arrived: {known} " f"(rssi: {rssi} dbm)") departed = known_nearby - current for d in departed: print(f"[{ts}] left: {d}") # summary all_count = len(devices) tracked = ", ".join(current) if current else "none" print(f"[{ts}] {all_count} ble devices nearby | " f"tracked: {tracked}") known_nearby = current await asyncio.sleep(10) if __name__ == "__main__": asyncio.run(scan_loop()) rssi-based distance estimation ble rssi correlates with distance better than wifi because ble uses lower transmit power and shorter wavelength. a rough model: import math def estimate_distance(rssi, tx_power=-59): """estimate distance in meters from ble rssi. tx_power: rssi at 1 meter (typical: -59 for phones) returns distance in meters. accuracy: +/- 2m indoors.""" if rssi == 0: return -1 ratio = rssi / tx_power if ratio this is a rough estimate. indoors, walls and reflections make ble distance estimation accurate to maybe 2-3 meters. outdoors with line of sight, you can get within 1 meter. don't rely on this for anything precision-critical. the mac randomization problem (again) just like wifi probe requests, ble advertisements use randomized mac addresses. you can still identify devices by their advertised name (which many devices include), but unnamed devices can't be tracked across sessions. apple devices rotate their ble address every 15 minutes. workaround: most personal devices (airpods, apple watch, fitness trackers) include a recognizable device name in their ble advertisements. track by name, not by mac. 7. why csi doesn't work on your mac this is worth explaining properly because it's the most common question people have when they read about wifi sensing. what csi requires at the hardware level when your wifi chip receives a frame, it internally computes the channel state information to decode the data. this computation happens in the chip's firmware. the chip uses csi to figure out how to decode the signal, then throws the csi away. it never passes it to the operating system because the os doesn't need it for normal wifi operation. to extract csi, you need to modify the firmware to save and export this data instead of discarding it. this is what the nexmon project does for broadcom chips, and what intel's csi tool does for the intel 5300. why macos blocks this three layers of lockout: chip support: modern macbooks (2020+) use the broadcom bcm4387. nexmon has firmware patches for many broadcom chips (bcm4339, bcm4358, bcm43455c0 in raspberry pi), but not the bcm4387. nobody has reverse-engineered this chip's firmware format. sip (system integrity protection): even if you had a bcm4387 firmware patch, macos prevents loading modified firmware. sip blocks modifications to system-level components, including wifi driver firmware. disabling sip is possible but apple increasingly ties system features to sip being enabled. driver integration: apple's wifi driver (airportbrcmnic) is tightly integrated with the hardware. it's not the same driver as the open-source brcmfmac used on linux. a firmware patch that works with the linux driver won't work with apple's driver. what about external usb wifi adapters? theoretically, you could plug in a usb wifi adapter with a supported broadcom or atheros chip and run csi extraction through it. in practice, macos driver support for third-party wifi adapters has been terrible since apple dropped the kext-based driver model. most usb wifi adapters don't work on modern macos at all, let alone with modified firmware. if you want csi on a mac, the realistic path is: buy an esp32 ($8), flash espectre, and read the data over the network. the esp32 handles all the rf processing. your mac just receives the binary motion/idle state via wifi (through home assistant, mqtt, or a simple http endpoint). full tools table tool github stars what it does works on mac? install whereami ~5k wifi fingerprint room detection yes pip3 install whereami find3 ~4.7k multi-device indoor positioning yes (client) docker + cli binary bleak ~1.8k cross-platform ble scanning yes pip3 install bleak nmap ~10k network discovery and arp scanning yes brew install nmap scapy ~10k+ packet crafting and arp scanning yes (sudo) pip3 install scapy ruview ~39k wifi csi visualization no (needs csi) n/a on mac espectre growing esp32 csi motion detection no (esp32 only) esphome flash nexmon_csi ~700 broadcom csi extraction no (linux/rpi) n/a on mac security concerns every detection method has security and ethical implications. here's the honest breakdown: arp scanning risk: low. you're querying your own network. anyone on the same lan could do this. it's equivalent to looking at your router's connected devices page. don't scan networks you don't own. rssi monitoring risk: minimal. you're reading your own wifi connection's signal strength. no third-party data is captured. this is information your laptop already has and uses internally. probe request sniffing risk: moderate. you're passively capturing wireless transmissions from other people's devices. in most jurisdictions, passively receiving radio signals is legal (you're not decrypting anything), but using the data to track individuals may violate privacy laws. gdpr considers mac addresses personal data. in your own home for your own use, the risk is low. in a public space or for commercial purposes, consult a lawyer. wifi fingerprinting risk: low. you're scanning signal strengths of nearby access points. this is the same data your laptop uses to choose which network to connect to. you're not accessing any other network or capturing any traffic. router-based risk: low for your own router. you're querying your own equipment's admin api. accessing someone else's router api without authorization is unauthorized computer access. don't do that. ble scanning risk: moderate. similar to probe sniffing. you're receiving publicly broadcast ble advertisements. the data is intentionally transmitted by the devices. but using it to track individuals (especially in public spaces) raises the same privacy concerns as probe sniffing. general principle on your own network, in your own home, for your own devices: all of these are fine. the moment you start tracking other people's devices without their knowledge, you're in ethically and potentially legally questionable territory. the technology doesn't care about your intentions. the law does. what to actually use if you're reading this and want to set something up today, here's the decision tree: goal: know who's home use arp scanning . map your family members' phone mac addresses, scan every 30 seconds, done. ten minutes to set up, 95% reliable. goal: know which room you're in use whereami . train it on each room, run it in the background. 30 minutes to train, 85-95% accurate. this is the best no-hardware-required option for room-level awareness. goal: detect motion in a room use rssi monitoring as a rough indicator, but honestly, buy an esp32 for $8 and run espectre. the laptop-only rssi approach is a proof of concept, not a reliable system. csi-based detection on an esp32 is 99%+ accurate. rssi monitoring on a laptop is 60-75%. goal: detect nearby people (not on your network) use ble scanning with bleak. it'll pick up phones, watches, earbuds, and fitness trackers within 10-15 meters. you won't know whose they are (mac randomization), but you'll know someone's nearby. goal: build a real presence detection system combine arp scanning (who's home) + whereami (which room) + an esp32 with espectre (is someone actually in this room right now). layer the methods. each covers a gap the others miss. total hardware cost: $8 for the esp32. total software cost: $0. for the full technical landscape including esp32 csi hardware, the espectre processing pipeline, the march 2026 laptop-only sensing breakthrough, and home assistant integration, read the main article: wifi can see you move . fnsmdehip · github.com/fnsmdehip · 2026

subcarriers and ofdm amplitude vs phase multipath and the human body espectre deep dive architecture processing pipeline performance numbers ml detector 7 approaches ranked arp scanning rssi monitoring probe request sniffing wifi fingerprinting router-based detection csi extraction ble scanning the macos problem privacy: the killer angle tools and repos hardware you need the march 2026 breakthrough home assistant integration real use cases companion: macbook-only guide wifi can see you move: a technical guide to camera-free motion detection 2026-03-28 · series: wifi sensing your router already knows when you walk into a room. here's how to use that. the premise every wifi router in your house is constantly shouting radio waves into every room. those waves bounce off walls, furniture, and you. when you move, the bounce pattern changes. if you can measure that change precisely enough, you've built a motion detector that works through walls, in complete darkness, without a single camera. this isn't speculative. commodity hardware can do it today for under $10. the question is which approach to use, what the tradeoffs are, and how far the technology actually goes. this article covers all of it. what csi actually is most people know wifi signal strength as a single number: those bars on your phone, or the rssi value your router reports. channel state information is the high-resolution version. instead of one number, you get a matrix of complex values describing how the wireless channel behaves at each individual frequency slice. subcarriers and ofdm modern wifi (802.11n and later) uses ofdm: orthogonal frequency-division multiplexing. instead of transmitting on one frequency, it splits the channel into many narrow sub-channels called subcarriers. in ht20 mode (20 mhz bandwidth), there are 64 subcarriers, though not all carry data. roughly 52 are data subcarriers, 4 are pilots, and the rest are guard bands and a dc null. each subcarrier is a complex number: h(f) = |h(f)| * e^(j*angle(h(f))). the magnitude |h(f)| tells you how much the signal was attenuated at that frequency. the phase angle tells you how much the signal was delayed. together, they describe the complete transfer function of the wireless channel at that frequency. when a standard wifi frame arrives, the receiver estimates these values for every subcarrier to properly decode the data. csi sensing hijacks this process. you don't care about the data in the frame. you care about how the channel itself changed since the last frame. amplitude vs phase amplitude data is the workhorse for most wifi sensing. it's relatively stable, less sensitive to clock synchronization issues, and easier to process on embedded hardware. when someone walks through a room, you'll see amplitude fluctuations in the 0.5-10 hz range across multiple subcarriers simultaneously. phase data is more sensitive but harder to use. the problem is phase offset: the transmitter and receiver have independent oscillators that drift relative to each other, introducing a random phase shift that varies across subcarriers. you can partially fix this with phase calibration (using pilot subcarriers as references), but on esp32 hardware without external clock synchronization, amplitude-based methods are more reliable. the research frontier is using both. phase carries information that amplitude misses, especially for fine-grained gesture recognition. but for binary presence detection (someone's here vs. nobody's here), amplitude alone gets you to 99%+ accuracy. multipath and the human body here's the physics that makes this work. a wifi signal doesn't travel in a straight line from router to receiver. it bounces off every surface: walls, floors, ceiling, furniture, that stack of books you'll never read. each reflection arrives at the receiver with a different delay, amplitude, and phase. the receiver sees the sum of all these paths. the human body is roughly 60% water. water absorbs 2.4 ghz radio waves. when you stand in a room, you're simultaneously absorbing energy from some paths and reflecting energy along new paths. when you move, you change which paths exist and how strong they are. the csi matrix shifts. the key insight: different subcarriers are affected differently by the same movement. a subcarrier at the edge of the band might see a huge amplitude swing while one in the center barely changes. by looking at the pattern of changes across all subcarriers simultaneously, you get far more information than any single measurement. espectre deep dive espectre is the most mature open-source implementation of wifi csi sensing on esp32 hardware. it's built as an esphome component, which means it integrates directly into home assistant with zero custom code. architecture the system runs entirely on the esp32 microcontroller. no cloud. no server. no external processing. the esp32 listens to wifi traffic from your existing router (or a dedicated access point) and extracts csi data from every received frame. processing happens on-chip in real time. the detection pipeline has two algorithms you can choose between: mvs (moving variance segmentation): statistical approach. no training required. works out of the box. uses the variance of spatial turbulence over a sliding window to distinguish motion from stillness. ml detector: a compact neural network (353 parameters, 1.4 kb) embedded directly in firmware. extracts 12 statistical features from the turbulence buffer and classifies them through two hidden layers. needs training data but achieves 100% accuracy on several esp32 variants. processing pipeline on boot, the system runs a 10.5-second calibration sequence: stage duration packets purpose gain lock 3.0s 300 lock agc and fft gain using median of samples. eliminates hardware-induced amplitude variations. nbvi calibration 7.5s 750 select 12 optimal subcarriers from 64 available using normalized baseline variability index. after calibration, every incoming packet goes through this chain: raw i/q values | v amplitude extraction (for each of 12 selected subcarriers) | v spatial turbulence = cv (std_dev / mean) across subcarriers | v [optional] hampel filter: replaces outliers > 4x mad with median | v [optional] low-pass butterworth iir, 11 hz cutoff | v moving variance over sliding window (75 packets = 0.75s at 100 pps) | v adaptive threshold comparison (95th percentile of baseline * 1.1) | v binary output: idle or motion the spatial turbulence metric uses coefficient of variation (cv = standard deviation / mean), which is mathematically gain-invariant. if the receiver's agc scales all amplitudes by factor k, the cv is unchanged. this is what makes the system robust across different esp32 boards with different rf front-end characteristics. nbvi subcarrier selection not all 64 subcarriers are useful. guard bands have no data. the dc subcarrier is always zero. some subcarriers have poor snr in your specific environment. nbvi scores each candidate subcarrier on a weighted combination of stability (low variance at rest) and signal strength (high mean amplitude): nbvi(i) = alpha * (sigma_i / mu_i^2) + (1 - alpha) * (sigma_i / mu_i) // alpha = 0.5 balances stability vs signal strength // lower nbvi = better subcarrier for sensing the algorithm selects the 12 subcarriers with the lowest nbvi scores, enforcing a minimum gap of 2 indices between selections to avoid correlated measurements. calibration runs in 30-50ms. that's fast enough to recalibrate on the fly if the environment changes. hampel filter wifi environments produce occasional spikes: a microwave turning on, a bluetooth device hopping frequencies, electrical interference. the hampel filter catches these. for each turbulence sample, it computes the median of the local window, then the median absolute deviation (mad). if the sample deviates from the median by more than 4x the scaled mad, it's replaced with the median. disabled by default because most environments don't need it. low-pass filter a 1st-order butterworth iir filter with 11 hz cutoff. human movement generates signal variations in the 0.5-10 hz range. rf noise is typically above 15 hz. the filter strips the noise floor. also disabled by default since the mvs algorithm's moving variance already smooths high-frequency noise. performance numbers these are measured numbers from the espectre validation suite, not theoretical: esp32 variant mvs accuracy ml accuracy detection latency price point esp32 (original) 99.2% 100% ~310 us $5-8 esp32-s2 99.5% 100% ~260 us $4-6 esp32-s3 99.7% 100% ~150 us $8-10 esp32-c3 98.8% 99.4% ~380 us $3-5 esp32-c6 99.3% 100% ~200 us $4-6 detection latency means time from a person starting to move to the system outputting motion. at 150-440 microseconds, this is faster than your light switch. the ml detector hits 100% accuracy on most chips because the feature extraction captures patterns that the raw threshold comparison misses. these numbers come from controlled environments with single-person detection at 3-8 meter range. real-world deployment adds noise: pets, fans, hvac systems. espectre handles most of these with the adaptive threshold, but a ceiling fan directly between the router and esp32 will degrade performance. ml detector deep dive the neural network is deliberately tiny. two hidden layers: 16 neurons, then 8 neurons, both with relu activation. sigmoid output. total: 353 trainable parameters. the entire model fits in 1.4 kb and runs inference in under 100 microseconds on the esp32's tensilica core. the 12 input features extracted from the turbulence buffer: mean of turbulence values standard deviation maximum value minimum value zero-crossing rate: how often the signal crosses its mean skewness: asymmetry of the distribution kurtosis: tail heaviness shannon entropy: information content lag-1 autocorrelation: temporal persistence median absolute deviation linear regression slope: trend direction start-to-end delta: overall change training used 5-fold stratified cross-validation on 13,711 labeled samples. the model was validated against deliberately adversarial scenarios: fan oscillation, pet movement, door opening from another room, hvac cycling. it learned to distinguish these from human movement, which is why it outperforms the statistical mvs approach on edge cases. one practical advantage: the ml detector uses fixed subcarriers (indices 6, 12, 18, 24, 30, 36, 42, 48 plus four more), skipping the nbvi calibration phase entirely. boot time drops from 10.5 seconds to about 3 seconds. 7 approaches to wifi sensing, ranked csi is the most powerful approach, but it's not the only one. here's every method for detecting people using wireless signals, ranked from simplest to most capable. the companion article 7 ways to detect people with just your macbook covers the laptop-only methods in depth with working code. 1. arp scanning (simplest) how it works: send arp requests to your local network. if a device responds, someone's phone (and therefore someone) is home. nmap can do a full subnet scan in 2-3 seconds. accuracy: detects presence at home level. cannot detect room-level movement. fails if someone leaves their phone charging and goes out. latency: 2-10 seconds per scan cycle. privacy: you're querying your own network. low concern. tools: nmap -sn 192.168.1.0/24 , python scapy # quick arp scan with scapy from scapy.all import arp, ether, srp def scan_network(subnet="192.168.1.0/24"): ans, _ = srp( ether(dst="ff:ff:ff:ff:ff:ff") / arp(pdst=subnet), timeout=2, verbose=false ) return [(r.psrc, r.hwsrc) for _, r in ans] devices = scan_network() print(f"{len(devices)} devices online") for ip, mac in devices: print(f" {ip:16s} {mac}") 2. rssi monitoring how it works: continuously read the signal strength of your wifi connection. when someone walks between your device and the router, rssi drops. when they leave, it recovers. monitor the fluctuation pattern over time. accuracy: room-level presence detection. works best in small rooms where a person significantly alters the signal path. false positive rate climbs in large open spaces. latency: 1-5 seconds depending on polling rate. privacy: you're reading your own connection's signal strength. minimal concern. tools: macos corewlan framework, linux iwconfig , python subprocess # macos rssi monitoring loop import subprocess, time, statistics def get_rssi(): result = subprocess.run( ["/system/library/privateframeworks/apple80211.framework/" "versions/current/resources/airport", "-i"], capture_output=true, text=true ) for line in result.stdout.splitlines(): if "agrctlrssi" in line: return int(line.split(":")[1].strip()) return none readings = [] while true: rssi = get_rssi() if rssi: readings.append(rssi) if len(readings) > 30: readings.pop(0) if len(readings) >= 10: stdev = statistics.stdev(readings[-10:]) state = "motion" if stdev > 2.0 else "idle" print(f"rssi: {rssi:4d} dbm | stdev(10): {stdev:.2f} | {state}") time.sleep(0.5) 3. probe request sniffing how it works: wifi devices periodically broadcast probe requests looking for known networks. put your wifi adapter in monitor mode and listen. each probe contains a mac address (increasingly randomized) and sometimes the ssid the device is looking for. accuracy: detects device presence, not person presence. mac randomization (on by default in ios 14+ and android 10+) severely limits tracking. you'll see a device but may not be able to identify whose it is across sessions. latency: seconds to minutes. devices don't probe continuously. privacy: significant concern. you're passively capturing other people's device transmissions. legal in most jurisdictions for your own property, but grey area for public spaces. gdpr considers mac addresses personal data. tools: tshark , tcpdump , airmon-ng . macos has limited monitor mode support on newer chips. 4. wifi fingerprinting (whereami) how it works: scan all visible access points and record their signal strengths. each room in your house has a unique "fingerprint" because walls attenuate different aps differently. train a classifier on room-labeled scans, then classify new scans in real time. accuracy: room-level location (which room you're in), not motion detection. 85-95% accuracy after good training. degrades if you rearrange furniture or add/remove aps. latency: 3-5 seconds per classification. privacy: you're scanning your own environment. low concern. tools: whereami (5k stars), find3 (4.7k stars) 5. router-based detection how it works: some routers expose connected device lists, bandwidth usage, and connection events via apis or web interfaces. query these to see who's connected, when they connected, and how much data they're using. accuracy: home-level presence. better than arp scanning because routers track connection/disconnection events with timestamps. latency: router-dependent. some update device lists every 30 seconds, others every 5 minutes. privacy: you're querying your own router. low concern. tools: router-specific apis. openwrt has luci/ubus. unifi has a rest api. consumer routers vary wildly. 6. csi extraction (the real deal) how it works: extract the full channel state information matrix from wifi frames. analyze the per-subcarrier amplitude and phase changes to detect movement, classify activities, count people, and even recognize gestures. accuracy: 98-100% for presence detection with espectre. 85-95% for activity recognition in research settings. 70-80% for gesture classification. latency: 150-440 microseconds on esp32. real-time. privacy: best possible. no visual data, no audio, no personal identifiers. just rf channel statistics. hardware required: esp32 ($4-10), or intel 5300/atheros with modified firmware on linux, or nexmon-patched broadcom on raspberry pi. does not work on macos natively. tools: espectre , esp32-csi-tool 7. ble scanning how it works: scan for bluetooth low energy advertisements. most modern devices (phones, watches, earbuds, fitness trackers) periodically broadcast ble advertisements even when not actively paired. accuracy: device-level presence. better range fingerprinting than wifi because ble rssi attenuates more predictably with distance. ibeacon and eddystone protocols provide sub-meter positioning. latency: 1-10 seconds depending on advertising interval. privacy: same concerns as probe request sniffing. you're listening to other devices' broadcasts. mac randomization applies here too. tools: python bleak library, bluetoothctl on linux the macos problem: why your macbook can't do csi if you're on a modern macbook (2020 or later), you have a broadcom bcm4387 wifi chip. this chip is perfectly capable of csi extraction at the hardware level. the problem is software. csi extraction requires patching the wifi firmware to expose the per-subcarrier channel estimates that the chip computes internally but never reports to the os. on linux, the nexmon project provides firmware patches for many broadcom chips, including older ones found in raspberry pi 3/4. but nexmon doesn't support the bcm4387. even if someone wrote a bcm4387 patch, macos has system integrity protection (sip). sip prevents loading unsigned kernel extensions and modifying system firmware. you'd need to disable sip, write a custom firmware patch for an undocumented chip, and hope apple's wi-fi driver accepts the modified firmware. in practice, nobody has done this. the situation is different on linux. intel 5300 and atheros chips have well-supported csi tools. the nexmon project covers many broadcom chips used in raspberry pi. but on macos with apple's specific broadcom integration, you're locked out. that's why the companion article focuses on the 6 approaches that do work on a macbook without any extra hardware. privacy: the killer angle this is why wifi sensing matters more than the technical novelty suggests. camera-based smart home systems have a fundamental problem: they capture video. that video can be hacked, subpoenaed, sold to data brokers, analyzed by ai for things you never consented to, or simply leaked. ring doorbells have been accessed by law enforcement without warrants. nest cameras have been compromised. every camera in your house is a potential surveillance endpoint. wifi csi sensing captures none of this. there is no visual data. there is no audio. there are no biometrics. the raw data is a stream of numbers describing how radio waves bounce around a room. you cannot reconstruct what someone looks like, what they're wearing, who they are, or what they're saying from csi data. what you can determine: someone is in the room (presence) they're moving or still (motion) roughly how many people are present (emerging research) coarse activity classification: walking, sitting, lying down (research-grade) whether someone has fallen and isn't getting up (elderly care) what you cannot determine: who the person is what they look like what they're saying what they're wearing what's on their screen any biometric information for home automation, elderly monitoring, and security, this is exactly the right tradeoff. you get the occupancy data you need for automation without creating a surveillance system that could be turned against you. tools and repos tool stars purpose hardware ruview ~39k wifi csi visualization and analysis toolkit various whereami ~5k wifi fingerprint room detection. python cli. any laptop find3 ~4.7k indoor positioning framework. multi-device. any wifi device espectre growing csi motion detection for esp32. esphome native. esp32 variants esp32-csi-tool ~600 raw csi data collection on esp32 esp32 nexmon_csi ~700 csi extraction via nexmon firmware patches rpi 3/4, select broadcom bleak ~1.8k cross-platform ble scanning library any bluetooth adapter hardware you need for full csi sensing, you need an esp32 board. here's the quick buyer's guide: board price best for notes esp32-s3 devkit $8-10 best overall performance 150 us detection, dual-core 240 mhz, 512 kb sram esp32-c6 devkit $4-6 best value wifi 6 support, 200 us detection, risc-v core esp32-c3 supermini $3-5 cheapest option single risc-v core, 380 us detection, slightly lower accuracy esp32 (original) $5-8 maximum compatibility most documentation, largest community you also need a wifi source. your existing home router works. the esp32 listens to the router's beacon frames (sent 10 times per second by default) and extracts csi from each one. optimal placement: esp32 3-8 meters from the router, with the area you want to monitor between them. total cost for a single-room sensor: $4-10 for the esp32 plus a usb cable and power adapter you probably already have. compare that to a ring indoor cam at $60 or a nest cam at $100, both of which require cloud subscriptions for full functionality. the march 2026 breakthrough in march 2026, a research paper (arxiv:2603.10845) demonstrated laptop-only wifi sensing without custom hardware or firmware modifications. the approach uses standard wifi management frames that any wifi adapter can receive, extracting motion signatures from timing variations and signal strength patterns across multiple access points. the key insight: you don't need per-subcarrier csi if you have enough access points. by correlating rssi changes across 3+ aps visible from a single location, you can triangulate motion with room-level accuracy. the paper reported 91% presence detection accuracy using just a standard laptop wifi adapter in a typical apartment with 4-6 visible networks. this is significant because it removes the hardware barrier entirely. no esp32 needed, no firmware patches, no special chips. any laptop running linux or macos can implement this with standard wifi scanning apis. the tradeoff is lower accuracy (91% vs 99%+) and higher latency (3-5 seconds vs microseconds). but for home automation triggers, 91% at 3-second latency is entirely usable. home assistant integration espectre is built as a native esphome component. if you're already running home assistant, setup is yaml configuration: # esphome yaml for espectre motion sensor esphome: name: room-presence platform: esp32 board: esp32-s3-devkitc-1 wifi: ssid: !secret wifi_ssid password: !secret wifi_password external_components: - source: github://francescopace/espectre binary_sensor: - platform: espectre name: "living room presence" device_class: motion # optional: choose detector detector: mvs # or "ml" for neural network # optional: sensitivity tuning threshold_multiplier: 1.1 window_size: 75 once connected, the sensor appears in home assistant as a standard binary motion sensor. you can use it in automations exactly like a pir sensor or camera-based motion detector: turn on lights when someone enters a room turn off hvac when rooms are unoccupied for 15 minutes send alerts if motion is detected while everyone's supposed to be away track occupancy patterns over time for energy optimization the advantage over pir (passive infrared) sensors: wifi csi detects stationary presence. if you're sitting still at your desk reading, a pir sensor thinks the room is empty after its timeout. csi continues to detect your presence because your breathing alone creates measurable signal variations. real use cases elderly care and fall detection this is the highest-impact application. a wifi csi sensor in each room of an elderly person's home can detect: normal activity patterns, prolonged stillness that might indicate a fall, bathroom visits at unusual hours that might indicate health changes, and general daily activity levels. all without a single camera, without the person needing to wear anything, and without any dignity-compromising surveillance. if someone falls and doesn't get back up within a configurable timeout, the system can alert a caregiver. unlike a wearable fall detector, it works even if the person forgot to put it on. whole-home energy management hvac is typically 40-60% of home energy costs. with room-level occupancy sensing, you can heat and cool only occupied rooms. a deployment of 5-6 esp32 sensors ($30-60 total) can reduce hvac costs by 15-30% in homes with zoned systems. payback period: 1-3 months. security without surveillance a wifi csi sensor can tell you someone is in your house. it can't tell you it's your kid coming home from school vs. a burglar. but combined with a door/window sensor and a smart lock, you get a layered system: authorized entry (smart lock code) + motion tracking (csi) covers most residential security needs without any cameras. smart lighting and automation the stationary detection capability is the game changer here. current smart home motion sensors (pir) have a timeout problem: you're reading on the couch, the lights turn off because you haven't moved in 10 minutes, you wave your arms to trigger the sensor. csi sensing eliminates this by detecting the micro-movements of breathing and subtle body shifts that pir misses entirely. companion article want to try wifi sensing with just your laptop? the companion piece 7 ways to detect people with just your macbook covers every approach that works without extra hardware, including working python code for arp scanning, rssi monitoring, wifi fingerprinting, and ble scanning. fnsmdehip ·
